title: Designing Multi-Agent Orchestration Pipelines
summary: A canonical procedure for designing multi-agent systems with explicit role separation, typed handoff contracts, shared state governance, and conflict resolution policy. Produces a pipeline specification that is deterministic, auditable, and composable. Root orchestration primitive — task decomposition, evaluation, and memory architectures derive from it.
preconditions: The task requires more than one capability domain or execution phase.
A single agent context window or capability set is insufficient for the full task.

cost_estimate.notes: Role decomposition and conflict resolution selection require domain judgment. Contract specification and instrumentation are templatable and partially automatable.

steps.action: Enumerate the full task scope. Identify natural phase boundaries where the output of one phase becomes the typed input to the next.
steps.rationale: A boundary is only valid if it can be expressed as a named contract. If you cannot write 'Phase A produces X, Phase B consumes X', the boundary is wrong and agents will couple implicitly through shared assumptions. Implicit coupling breaks silently under load or model changes — it is the primary source of non-deterministic failures in multi-agent systems.

tooling.automated: Prompt: 'Given this task: {task_description}, list the execution phases in order. For each phase output JSON: [{phase_id, description, input_artifact_name, output_artifact_name}]. Flag any phase where input or output cannot be named.'
tooling.manual: Draw the pipeline as boxes and arrows. Every arrow must have a name. If you cannot name an arrow, the phases are wrong — merge or re-split until every boundary is nameable.

steps.action: Assign exactly one role per agent from: Planner, Executor, Critic, Router, or Aggregator. No agent holds two roles.
steps.rationale: Dual-role agents create untestable behaviour — you cannot isolate which role caused a failure. The five roles are mutually exclusive by design. Any phase that seems to need a dual-role agent is a signal to split into two agents.

tooling.automated: Prompt: 'Map each phase in {task_scope_map} to exactly one of: Planner | Executor | Critic | Router | Aggregator. Output JSON: [{phase_id, agent_id, role, justification}]. Flag any ambiguous mapping.'
tooling.manual: Role decision rules — Planner: output is a task list or plan. Executor: output is a concrete artifact (code, text, data). Critic: output is a boolean + reason. Router: output is a dispatch decision with destination. Aggregator: output is a merge of N inputs into one.

steps.action: Define the handoff contract for every agent boundary: input schema, output schema, error schema, and timeout SLA.
steps.rationale: The handoff contract is the only interface between agents. Without it, agents couple through shared assumptions. Any agent change that alters the output schema without bumping the contract version is a silent breaking change.

tooling.automated: Contract template: { boundary_id, from_agent_id, to_agent_id, input: { required: [{name, type, description}], optional: [{name, type, description}] }, output: { required: [{name, type, description}], optional: [{name, type, description}] }, error: { codes: [{code, meaning}], schema: {} }, timeout_ms: number, version: '1.0.0' }
tooling.manual: For each arrow in task_scope_map: write the contract. For each field ask: what happens if this field is absent? If the downstream agent breaks, the field is required. If it degrades gracefully, it is optional. Version the contract with semver — any removal of a required field is a major bump.

steps.action: Specify the shared state store: enumerate every shared key, assign exactly one writer per key, and declare the consistency model.
steps.rationale: Multiple agents writing the same key without coordination produces non-deterministic output. The root cause of most multi-agent data corruption bugs is unintended write contention. The invariant is: one writer per key, all others are readers.

tooling.automated: State spec template per key: { key, description, writer_agent_id, reader_agent_ids: [], consistency: 'append-only' | 'last-writer-wins' | 'critic-arbitrated', conflict_ttl_ms: number }. Default to append-only wherever possible.
tooling.manual: Decision tree — Can the key be append-only (event log)? Yes → use it. No, conflicts are rare and eventual consistency is OK → last-writer-wins. No, conflicts affect downstream decisions → critic-arbitrated. Document the choice with a one-line justification per key.

steps.action: Select conflict resolution strategy: last-writer-wins, Critic-arbitrated, or human-in-the-loop. Document the consistency trade-off accepted.
steps.rationale: Every multi-agent system will encounter a conflict. Choosing the strategy at design time prevents ad-hoc fixes under production pressure. The strategy is a consistency vs. latency trade-off that should be revisitable — so document the reasoning, not just the decision.

tooling.automated: Selection matrix: last-writer-wins → conflict_rate_estimate < 5% AND downstream_sensitivity = low. Critic-arbitrated → conflict_rate > 5% OR downstream_sensitivity = high. Human-in-the-loop → regulatory_requirement = true OR action_is_irreversible = true.
tooling.manual: Conflict simulation: inject two agents writing conflicting values to the same key simultaneously. Trace the output. If acceptable, the strategy is validated. If not, move up the consistency ladder (LWW → Critic → Human).

steps.action: Define termination conditions: success, failure, and partial-failure rollback procedure.
steps.rationale: A pipeline without explicit termination conditions never cleans up after partial failure. The most common production incident pattern in multi-agent systems is a partial failure that leaves shared state in an inconsistent intermediate state, blocking all subsequent runs indefinitely.

tooling.automated: Termination spec template: { success: 'all handoff_contracts satisfied AND aggregator output passes Critic validation', failure: 'any agent exceeds timeout_ms OR emits error_schema', partial_failure: { action: 'cancel in-flight tasks', emit: 'last_valid_partial_result', log: 'trace_id + boundary_id of failure' } }
tooling.manual: Test rollback: kill an Executor mid-run. Verify the pipeline terminates cleanly with the partial result. If it hangs or emits corrupted state, the termination spec is incomplete. Fix before proceeding.

steps.action: Instrument every handoff boundary: emit a structured trace event with agent_id, role, input_hash, output_hash, latency_ms, and status.
steps.rationale: Without structured traces at boundaries, debugging a failure requires reconstructing execution order from unstructured logs — unreliable under concurrent execution. The trace event is the minimal observable unit. It enables replay, diff-based regression testing, and root-cause isolation to a specific boundary. It also feeds the evaluation pipeline (KB-AG-3) for regression detection.

tooling.automated: Trace event schema: { trace_id: uuid, span_id: uuid, parent_span_id: uuid | null, boundary_id: string, from_agent_id: string, to_agent_id: string, input_hash: 'keccak256(JSON.stringify(input))', output_hash: 'keccak256(JSON.stringify(output))', latency_ms: number, status: 'ok' | 'error' | 'timeout', error_code: string | null, timestamp: ISO8601 }
tooling.manual: Completeness check: replay a known failure scenario and confirm you can identify the failing boundary from traces alone — without reading agent logs or source code. If you cannot isolate the boundary from traces, add fields until you can.

successConditions: Every agent in role_assignment_map holds exactly one of the five defined roles
Every boundary in task_scope_map has a versioned handoff_contract with input, output, and error schemas
Every shared state key has exactly one writer_agent_id in shared_state_spec
conflict_resolution_policy is selected with a documented consistency trade-off
termination_spec covers success, failure, and partial_failure with tested rollback
instrumentation_spec defines trace events at every handoff boundary
failureConditions: Any agent holds two roles — cannot isolate failure cause in testing or production
Any handoff boundary lacks a typed contract — agents are implicitly coupled through assumptions
Multiple agents write to the same shared state key without coordination — non-deterministic output
No termination condition defined for partial failure — pipeline hangs indefinitely on partial failure
No trace events at boundaries — failures cannot be root-cause isolated without log reconstruction

inputs.name: task_description
inputs.description: Full natural language or structured description of the task the pipeline must complete

inputs.name: available_agents
inputs.description: Declared agent types with their capability sets

outputs.name: pipeline_spec
outputs.description: Complete specification: role_assignment_map, handoff_contracts, shared_state_spec, conflict_resolution_policy, termination_spec, instrumentation_spec

outputs.name: handoff_contracts
outputs.description: Typed, versioned contracts for every agent boundary

outputs.name: instrumentation_spec
outputs.description: Trace event schema for every handoff boundary
